title: Check Soil Before Watering
body: Stick a finger an inch into the soil before watering. If it's still damp, wait a day or two — overwatering is the most common way indoor plants die.

title: Rotate for Even Light
body: Give potted plants a quarter turn every week so all sides get even sunlight, keeping growth balanced instead of leaning toward the window.

title: Wipe Dust Off Leaves
body: Dusty leaves block light and slow photosynthesis. Wipe broad leaves with a damp cloth every few weeks to keep them breathing freely.

title: Empty the Drainage Saucer
body: Never let a pot sit in standing water. Empty the saucer 15-20 minutes after watering to prevent root rot.

title: Morning Watering Wins
body: Water in the morning when possible — it gives plants time to absorb moisture before the cooler night, reducing the risk of fungal growth.

title: Yellow Leaves Aren't Always Bad
body: An occasional yellow leaf near the base is often just natural aging. Only worry if yellowing spreads quickly across newer growth.

title: Brown Tips Mean Low Humidity
body: Crispy brown leaf tips usually point to dry air rather than underwatering. Try a pebble tray or grouping plants together to raise humidity.

title: Skip Fertilizer in Winter
body: Most houseplants slow their growth in winter and need little to no fertilizer. Resume feeding once new growth appears in spring.

title: Use Room-Temperature Water
body: Cold tap water can shock tropical plant roots. Let water sit out for a few hours, or use lukewarm water straight from the tap.

title: Prune Leggy Growth
body: Long, sparse stems reaching for light benefit from a trim just above a leaf node — it encourages fuller, bushier growth.

title: Repot When Roots Circle the Pot
body: If roots are winding tightly around the inside of the pot or poking out of drainage holes, it's time to move up one pot size.

title: Acclimate New Plants Slowly
body: A plant fresh from the nursery is used to controlled conditions. Introduce it to your home's light and airflow gradually over a week or two.

title: Mist Carefully, Not Constantly
body: Misting gives a brief humidity boost but evaporates fast. For lasting effect, use a humidifier or pebble tray instead of frequent misting alone.

title: Watch for Tiny Webs
body: Fine webbing between leaves and stems is a classic sign of spider mites. Isolate the plant and rinse leaves thoroughly to knock them back.

title: Bottom-Water Sensitive Plants
body: For plants prone to crown rot, set the pot in a tray of water for 10-15 minutes so roots soak moisture up from below instead of overhead.

title: Group Plants for Humidity
body: Clustering humidity-loving plants together creates a shared microclimate as they release moisture through their leaves.

title: Match Pot Size to Plant Size
body: A pot that's too large holds excess moisture the roots can't use, raising the risk of root rot. Size up gradually, not drastically.

title: Keep Leaves Away From Cold Glass
body: Leaves touching a cold windowpane in winter can suffer chill damage. Leave a small gap between foliage and the glass.

title: Sterilize Tools Between Plants
body: Wipe pruning shears with rubbing alcohol between plants to avoid spreading fungal or bacterial issues from one to another.

title: Direct Sun Can Scorch Leaves
body: Many popular houseplants prefer bright, indirect light. Harsh afternoon sun through a south-facing window can scorch tender leaves.

title: Flush Salts From Soil Occasionally
body: Mineral buildup from tap water and fertilizer can crust on the soil surface. Every couple of months, water thoroughly until it drains freely to flush it out.

title: New Growth Is a Good Health Sign
body: Fresh leaves or visible root growth mean your plant is settled and thriving — a more reliable health signal than leaf count alone.

title: Isolate New Plants First
body: Keep a newly bought plant away from the rest of your collection for a couple of weeks to catch any hidden pests before they spread.

title: Adjust Watering With the Seasons
body: Plants drink less during shorter, cooler days. Reduce watering frequency in autumn and winter, then increase it again as growth picks up in spring.
